column1: What is a variable's storage duration, and the two kinds?
column2: It determines the variable's lifetime and home. Static: globals, static locals, file-scope — exist for the whole program, one fixed address each in .data/.bss/.rodata. Automatic: ordinary locals and parameters — exist only while the function runs, on the stack or in registers.

column1: What is the stack and which way does it grow?
column2: A region of RAM that grows and shrinks as functions are called and return. On ARM and x86 it grows downward, toward lower addresses.
The slice of stack an active function owns — holding its saved registers, the return address, and any locals that could not stay in registers.

column1: What do a function's prologue and epilogue do?
column2: The prologue allocates the frame by subtracting from the stack pointer on entry. The epilogue releases it by restoring the stack pointer before returning.

column1: At -O0, where do local variables live?
column2: Every local gets a stack slot, written and re-read at each use — lots of str/ldr — so you can inspect them in a debugger.

column1: At -O2, where do local variables often live?
column2: Short-lived locals whose address is never taken are kept in registers, and constant-folding may remove them entirely — the function may have no stack frame at all.

column1: Why is the rule local variable lives on the stack only the unoptimised truth?
column2: With optimisation, locals can live entirely in registers and vanish through constant-folding, so the stack frame shrinks or disappears — which is why optimised code is faster but harder to single-step.

column1: Why might a debugger show optimised out at -O2?
column2: The local was promoted to a register or folded away, so it has no stack slot to read. The C semantics are unchanged — only the storage changed.

column1: How do you force a local to stay in real memory?
column2: Take its address (&x) or declare it volatile — registers have no address, so either forces it onto the stack and keeps it inspectable.

column1: Why must memory-mapped registers be volatile?
column2: Otherwise the optimiser would keep them in a register like any local, which for hardware is catastrophic — it would never re-read the live register value.

column1: How does RISC (ARM Cortex-M) tend to allocate locals?
column2: A load/store architecture with many registers (13-16), so arithmetic is register-only and the compiler keeps lots of locals in registers, touching memory rarely. AAPCS passes the first four args in r0-r3 and returns in r0.

column1: How does CISC (x86) tend to allocate locals?
column2: Few registers (8 on 32-bit) and instructions that operate directly on memory operands, so locals spill to the stack sooner. The 32-bit System V ABI even passed arguments on the stack by default.

column1: Why is stack overflow worse on a no-MMU MCU?
column2: On a PC it triggers a clean page fault. On a no-MMU MCU the stack grows past its region and silently overwrites .bss or the heap, giving bizarre symptoms far from the cause.

column1: What embedded rules follow from the stack-overflow hazard?
column2: Bound or ban recursion, avoid large local arrays (a 4 KB local buffer is 4 KB of stack), and budget per-task stacks carefully under an RTOS.

column1: Why is a large local array dangerous?
column2: A local array is allocated on the stack the instant the function is entered (e.g. uint8_t line[512] needs 512 bytes immediately), so it can overflow a small task stack before doing any useful work.
